overview: Dara Khosrowshahi argues that Uber's turnaround — from burning roughly $3 billion the year before its IPO to over $30 billion in revenue with profitability — came from disciplined focus: supply-led marketplace growth borrowed from watching Booking.com beat Expedia, cross-selling between rides and Eats until the compounding showed up in margins, and deliberately low take rates that leave money with drivers and ecosystem partners.

The episode's arc runs through Dara's apprenticeship in high-stakes dealmaking — presenting his model to Barry Diller during the Paramount hostile bid, holding the Expedia acquisition together through 9/11 — culminating in the Uber crucible: a secret CEO recruitment sparked by Daniel Ek, the Travis Kalanick–Benchmark power struggle, and the all-or-nothing conversion of high-vote shares that ended the control fight and let the company build.

takeaways.title: Supply-led growth was the Booking.com lesson
takeaways.detail: Booking beat Expedia by relentlessly building fragmented hotel supply, which improved conversion and pulled in demand. Dara applied the same playbook at Uber post-pandemic: rebuilding the 5.6-million driver and courier base drove down surge and ETAs, accelerating trip growth to 24% in Q1.

takeaways.title: Rides app is Eats' biggest acquisition channel
takeaways.detail: Uber gets more new Eats customers from the rides app than from Google, Meta, and Instagram combined, at a quarter of the cost, while Eats couriers upsell into driving. Three years of this machinery compounding is why Uber's margins now grow faster than competitors'.

takeaways.title: Converting high-vote shares ended Uber's power struggle
takeaways.detail: SoftBank's multi-billion-dollar investment only worked if every high-vote holder — Travis, Benchmark, all of them — agreed to flip to low-vote shares, an all-or-none negotiation Dara likens to an $80 billion prisoner's dilemma. It removed board control entirely and shifted the company from fighting over power to building.

takeaways.title: Low take rates are a durability strategy
takeaways.detail: Dara spent 13 years grinding Expedia's take rate from 25% to the teens and resists raising Uber's, even though a small bump would flatter any quarter. Last quarter driver and courier earnings grew 30% while gross bookings grew 22% — leaving money in the ecosystem keeps the growth lasting.

takeaways.title: Earners demand a higher duty of care
takeaways.detail: Uber admits it took drivers for granted during oversupply, A/B testing their pay experience like a consumer feature — equivalent to experimenting on employees' 401k match. Because drivers spend four to six hours a day in the app, they hit worst-case P95 failures weekly, forcing a culture change in how Uber builds for them.

chapters.title: Uber's transformation since the 2019 IPO
chapters.summary: Ben and David frame the stakes: Uber went from record pre-IPO losses to $30 billion in revenue, two complementary businesses, and modest profitability.
chapters.quote: we wouldn't have dreamed Uber could even get to break even

chapters.title: Closing the Expedia deal through 9/11
chapters.summary: IAC could have invoked the MAC clause to abandon the Expedia acquisition after September 11th; Barry Diller's conviction about travel carried the deal through.
chapters.quote: if there isn't travel, there isn't life

chapters.title: A junior analyst meets Barry Diller
chapters.summary: During QVC's hostile bid for Paramount, Diller demanded to see whoever built the model — revealing his insistence on unvarnished truth and launching Dara's career.
chapters.quote: I want to talk to the person running the numbers

chapters.title: Booking.com's supply-led playbook applied to Uber
chapters.summary: Dara translates the lesson from losing to Booking — go after fragmented supply — into Uber's post-pandemic driver recruitment strategy.
chapters.quote: It's 5.6 million drivers and couriers who are earning on our platform

chapters.title: The rides-to-Eats flywheel starts compounding
chapters.summary: Merging the technical teams built cross-promotion machinery whose effects were invisible for years, then emerged as structural margin advantage over competitors.
chapters.quote: our margins are growing faster than our competition

chapters.title: Who actually sets Uber's prices
chapters.summary: Uber's top economist argues the marketplace sets the spot price for labor, explaining both post-pandemic price increases and the current counter-cyclical price declines.
chapters.quote: we're not setting prices. The marketplace is setting its own price

chapters.title: Daniel Ek and the secret CEO search
chapters.summary: Dara rebuffed the headhunter until a drink at Sun Valley reframed the Uber job as a question of impact rather than comfort.
chapters.quote: Dara, since when is life about having fun?

chapters.title: Telling Barry Diller he might leave
chapters.summary: After hanging up in anger, Diller chose friendship over business interest — even editing the PowerPoint Dara presented to Uber's board.
chapters.quote: speaking as a friend, I understand why you're interested

chapters.title: SoftBank and the $80 billion prisoner's dilemma
chapters.summary: Getting SoftBank's investment required every high-vote shareholder to convert simultaneously — ending the Travis-Benchmark control battle as a secondary benefit.
chapters.quote: everyone agreed to essentially switch over high vote to low vote

chapters.title: How safe must a robot driver be?
chapters.summary: Dara argues the barrier to autonomy isn't just the last 2% of edge cases but an unanswerable question about what fatality rate society will accept from machines.
chapters.quote: how safe does a robot have to be?

chapters.title: Why Dara refuses to raise take rates
chapters.summary: Asked to choose between his businesses, Dara instead explains the core financial philosophy: torture the organization into efficiency so the take rate can stay low.

chapters.title: Building for earners, not just consumers
chapters.summary: Dara's closing argument: Uber's future depends on being the best platform for its 5.6 million earners, requiring a culture change from consumer-style experimentation to genuine duty of care.
chapters.quote: Uber was guilty of taking earners for granted
